nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered, shallow reef zones with sandy or rubble substrate; found on reef flats, lagoon slopes, and lower reef walls in moderate light conditions

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Target feeding 2–3 times per week with small meaty foods (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, small krill pieces, Reef-Roids, and similar LPS pellets or coral foods) is generally advised to support growth and coloration. Feeding tentacles are more reliably extended in low-light conditions or at night.

goodWith: most LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive sweeper-tentacle corals placed in direct contact (torch, galaxea)
large predatory fish that nip coral tissue

imageCandidates.notes: Aguleo; 2239x2352. Brilliant neon-green trumpet colony under reef lighting, sharp, whole colony fully inflated — the classic Candy Cane look hobbyists buy.
Caroline Lena Becker; 4796x3197. High-res, tack-sharp yellow-green colony against dark background showing trumpet corallite structure; different color morph.
Aaron Gustafson; 640x640. Fluorescent green polyps with blue-tipped feeding tentacles under actinic light; low res but shows the neon reef-light look. Non-recommended extra.
Aaron Gustafson; 640x640. Vibrant yellow-green colony under blue actinic lighting; low res but adds color variety. Non-recommended extra.
Haplochromis; 2000x1333. Sharp full teal-and-tan colony with green-centered corallites; good structural view of the branching trumpet growth.
Wmpearl, Waikiki Aquarium; 1876x1616. C. furcata, sharp peach/orange corallites with green mouths; CC0. Distinct warm color morph.
George Berninger Jr.; 1536x2048. C. furcata frag, sharp pink corallites with lime-green centers; small aquarium label in far background.
Brian Jeffery Beggerly; 4243x3456. Broad teal/blue colony under reef lighting; slightly soft but shows a large mature colony and teal morph.
Kate Osborne; 2816x2112. C. furcata in situ on a wild reef, green-centered trumpet corallites; shows natural habitat (blue underwater cast).
Debra Baker; ~1024px. C. furcata in situ, yellow/olive trumpet corallites macro; wild reference showing corallite detail.

summary: The Candy Cane Coral (also called Trumpet Coral or Bullseye Coral) is a large polyp stony coral from the Indo-Pacific, widely considered one of the more forgiving LPS corals for newcomers. It tends to handle moderate fluctuations in water chemistry and thrives under moderate, indirect lighting.
careNotes:
Caulastrea is a genus of large polyp stony (LPS) corals in the family Merulinidae, commonly sold in the aquarium trade under the names Candy Cane Coral or Trumpet Coral. The most commonly traded species is Caulastrea furcata (Dana, 1846), though C. echinulata and C. connata are also found in the hobby.

Caulastrea is widely considered one of the better beginner LPS corals, as it tends to be forgiving of moderate water chemistry fluctuations and does not demand the intense lighting or flow required by many SPS corals.

Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally advised, with most sources suggesting 50–150 PAR. New specimens are often best introduced to a lower-light area and moved higher only if they remain closed for extended periods.

Flow: Low to moderate, indirect water movement is preferred. High or direct flow has been reported to cause polyps to lose their fleshy, inflated appearance. Positioning where detritus does not accumulate on the coral is important.

Placement: Middle to lower sections of the tank on sandy substrate or stable rockwork. These corals have been documented occupying sheltered reef habitats in the wild rather than high-energy reef crests.

Aggression: Caulastrea has notably shorter sweeper tentacles than many LPS corals (reported around 4–5 cm reach), making it less aggressive than torch or hammer corals. Nonetheless, adequate spacing of several centimeters from neighboring corals is generally advised. It has been known to sting adjacent corals over time if placed too close.

Feeding: Target feeding 2–3 times per week with mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, small krill, or specialized LPS/coral foods is commonly recommended. Feeding has been reported to visibly increase growth rate. Feeding when the lights are dim or off tends to produce a more reliable tentacle response.

Colony growth: Caulastrea colonies grow by adding new branches, with polyps dividing (longitudinal fission) over time. In captivity, colonies typically reach 10–30 cm across depending on tank size, light, and feeding regimen.

Common health concerns: Brown jelly disease and tissue recession (RTN) are occasional concerns, typically associated with poor water quality or physical injury. Loss of stripe coloration is often an early indicator of suboptimal lighting or deteriorating water parameters.

Fragging: Straightforward — individual branches are cut using bone cutters or a coral saw and glued to frag plugs. Recovery is typically 1–2 weeks before trading or selling. Fragging is an effective propagation method widely practiced in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Fragging by cutting individual polyp branches is the standard propagation method in captivity and is considered very easy. Natural polyp splitting (longitudinal fission) also increases colony size over time. Broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not deliberately practiced in home aquaria.

primary.name: Extreme Corals – Candy Cane Coral Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary source: full care guide with explicit PAR (80–150), temperature (76–80°F / 24–27°C), salinity (1.024–1.026 SG), pH (8.1–8.4), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), calcium (400–450 ppm), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm), flow (moderate indirect), placement (mid-lower), feeding (2–3x/week mysis/brine/reef-roids), care level (beginner).

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog – Candy Cane Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 73–84°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400+ ppm), flow (low-moderate), placement (lower), care level (easy). Slightly wider temperature range than primary; upper bound of 84°F (29°C) considered an outlier — averaged down to 28°C max. Noted sweeper tentacles are comparatively short vs. other LPS.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Candy Cane Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR (50–150, optimal 50–70), flow (low), placement (bottom), alkalinity (7–9 dKH general, 10–11 for faster growth), calcium (~425 ppm), magnesium (~1350 ppm), feeding (2–3x/week spot feeding). PAR min averaged to 50 across sources.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory – Candy Cane Coral Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR (30–150, optimal 50–70), temperature (75–82°F / 24–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), calcium (350–420 ppm), magnesium (~1350 ppm), sweeper tentacle reach (~2 inches / 5 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific, Fiji to Australia). Calcium lower bound of 350 ppm treated as outlier; averaged upward to 380 ppm minimum.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Caulastraea (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: family Merulinidae, order Scleractinia, genus established by Dana 1846. Confirmed species list (C. furcata, C. echinulata, C. connata, C. curvata, C. tumida). Not used for husbandry parameters.
